questions.question: The Treaty of Versailles, signed in 1919, formally ended which war?
questions.explanation: The Treaty of Versailles was signed on June 28, 1919, formally ending World War I. It placed full blame for the war on Germany (the 'war guilt clause'), imposed massive reparations, stripped Germany of territory and its military, and dissolved the Austro-Hungarian and Ottoman empires. Many historians argue that the treaty's humiliating terms fueled resentment in Germany, contributing to the rise of Adolf Hitler and World War II.
questions.memory_hook: Treaty of Versailles 1919 = WWI ends. Its harsh terms against Germany planted the seeds of WWII. John Maynard Keynes warned at the time it would cause another war. He was right.

questions.question: In what year did Columbus first arrive in the Americas?
questions.explanation: Christopher Columbus landed in the Bahamas on October 12, 1492, on his first voyage west from Spain. He was attempting to find a sea route to Asia. Columbus made four voyages to the Americas but never acknowledged he had found a previously unknown continent — he died believing he had reached the East Indies. Amerigo Vespucci later recognized it as a new continent, which is why it's called America.
questions.memory_hook: Columbus 1492. He sailed the ocean blue. Never admitted he found a new continent — died thinking he'd reached Asia. Vespucci figured it out, and the continents got named after him instead.

questions.question: Which ancient Greek philosopher was forced to drink hemlock as a death sentence?
questions.explanation: Socrates (470-399 BC) was tried and convicted by an Athenian court on charges of impiety and corrupting the youth. He was ordered to drink hemlock, a poisonous plant. Socrates refused to flee or recant, seeing his execution as an opportunity to demonstrate his principles. His trial and death are described in Plato's dialogues. His philosophical method — asking probing questions — is the foundation of the Socratic method still used in law schools today.
questions.memory_hook: Socrates = hemlock death, 399 BC. Condemned for questioning everything. He could have fled but chose death over recanting. His student Plato wrote about it — and Plato taught Aristotle, who taught Alexander the Great.

questions.question: What was the name of the first successful powered airplane flight in 1903?
questions.explanation: Orville and Wilbur Wright achieved the first successful powered airplane flight on December 17, 1903, at Kitty Hawk, North Carolina. Their aircraft, called the Flyer (later Flyer I), made four flights that day, the longest lasting 59 seconds and covering 260 meters. Within 10 years of the Wright Brothers' flight, airplanes were being used in warfare.
questions.memory_hook: Wright Brothers' plane = the Flyer. Kitty Hawk, December 17, 1903. 59 seconds, 260 meters. In 66 years, humans went from that first flight to landing on the Moon. Neil Armstrong carried a piece of the Flyer's wing to the Moon.

questions.question: The Kentucky Derby, the first leg of horse racing's Triple Crown, is run at which distance?
questions.explanation: The Kentucky Derby is run at one and a quarter miles (10 furlongs) at Churchill Downs in Louisville, Kentucky. It has been held annually on the first Saturday in May since 1875. Along with the Preakness Stakes and Belmont Stakes, it forms the Triple Crown. Winning all three in the same year is one of the rarest feats in sport — only 13 horses have done it.
questions.memory_hook: Kentucky Derby = 1.25 miles, first Saturday in May, Churchill Downs. Three-year-old horses only — they never race the Derby again. Only 13 horses have won all three Triple Crown races.

questions.question: Which country won the first modern Olympic Games in 1896?
questions.explanation: The United States won the most gold medals at the first modern Olympics, held in Athens, Greece in 1896. American athletes dominated track and field events. The games featured 241 athletes from 14 nations, all male. The host nation Greece won the most total medals, but the US topped the gold medal count. The games were revived by French Baron Pierre de Coubertin.
questions.memory_hook: 1896 Athens = first modern Olympics. USA won most gold. Only men competed. 241 athletes from 14 nations — tiny by today's standards (10,000+ athletes now). Pierre de Coubertin revived them as a path to world peace.

questions.question: In which sport do teams compete for the Stanley Cup?
questions.explanation: The Stanley Cup is the championship trophy of the National Hockey League (NHL). It was donated by Governor General Lord Stanley of Preston in 1892, making it North America's oldest professional sports trophy. The Cup has a tradition of champions engraving their names on it — it now weighs about 35 pounds. Winning teams get to keep the Cup for a day, leading to legendary celebrations.
questions.memory_hook: Stanley Cup = NHL ice hockey. Oldest professional sports trophy in North America (1892). The Cup goes home with each player for a day — it has been dunked in pools, flown to Europe, and reportedly used as a cereal bowl.

questions.question: Which 1972 Francis Ford Coppola film won the Academy Award for Best Picture and is widely considered one of the greatest films ever made?
questions.explanation: The Godfather (1972) won Best Picture, Best Actor (Marlon Brando), and Best Adapted Screenplay at the 1973 Academy Awards. Based on Mario Puzo's novel, it launched one of cinema's most acclaimed trilogies and redefined American crime films.
questions.memory_hook: Brando made them an offer they couldn't refuse — the Oscar agreed.

questions.question: Which artist is known as the 'Queen of Pop'?
questions.explanation: Madonna (Madonna Louise Ciccone) earned the title 'Queen of Pop' through decades of reinvention and commercial dominance. Her career began in the early 1980s with hits like 'Holiday' and 'Like a Virgin,' and she has remained relevant across five decades. She has sold over 300 million records, making her the best-selling female music artist of all time.
questions.memory_hook: Madonna = Queen of Pop. 300 million records sold — the best-selling female artist ever. Reinvented herself every few years: disco, new wave, techno, Latin, electronic. Still performing in her 60s.

questions.question: The 1984 film Ghostbusters starred which comedic actor as Dr. Peter Venkman?
questions.explanation: Bill Murray played the sardonic, lazy Dr. Peter Venkman in Ghostbusters (1984), directed by Ivan Reitman. The film became one of the highest-grossing comedies ever made. Murray was famously difficult to get back for sequels and took decades to agree to Ghostbusters: Afterlife (2021). His deadpan delivery and improvised lines throughout the film became iconic.
questions.memory_hook: Bill Murray = Ghostbusters. He improvised most of his best lines. Refused to do sequels for 30+ years. When he finally agreed to Afterlife in 2021, it was a major event. 'Who you gonna call?' has been in the public consciousness for 40 years.

questions.question: The Tropic of Cancer and the Tropic of Capricorn mark the northern and southern limits of what?
questions.explanation: The Tropic of Cancer (approximately 23.5 degrees north) and the Tropic of Capricorn (approximately 23.5 degrees south) mark the limits of the tropical zone — the region where the Sun is directly overhead at least once per year. The area between the tropics receives more direct solar energy and is generally hotter and wetter than higher latitudes.
questions.memory_hook: Tropics = between Cancer and Capricorn. 23.5 degrees north and south. The Sun is directly overhead somewhere in this band every day of the year. Outside these lines, the Sun is never directly overhead — which is why higher latitudes have milder climates.

questions.question: What is the name of the force that keeps electrons orbiting the nucleus of an atom?
questions.explanation: Electrons are held in orbit around the atomic nucleus by the electromagnetic force. Electrons (negatively charged) are attracted to protons (positively charged) in the nucleus. The electromagnetic force is one of the four fundamental forces of nature, along with gravity, the strong nuclear force (which holds the nucleus together), and the weak nuclear force (responsible for radioactive decay).
questions.memory_hook: Electrons orbit because of electromagnetism. Opposite charges attract — negative electrons are pulled toward positive protons. The four fundamental forces: gravity, electromagnetism, strong nuclear, weak nuclear. Everything in the universe runs on these four.
